perturbation.original_text: "EXCLUSIVE CATEGORY" means those product and/or service categories identified on Exhibit A.
perturbation.changed_text: "EXCLUSIVE CATEGORY" means those product and/or service categories as agreed upon by both parties.
perturbation.explanation: By modifying the definition of 'EXCLUSIVE CATEGORY' from being identified on Exhibit A to being 'as agreed upon by both parties,' we remove the clear reference to Exhibit A. This omission creates an in-text contradiction because Section 2.6 still references the 'Exclusive Category' but without a clear pointer to Exhibit A. It now implies that the definition can be found elsewhere or doesn't explicitly exists, creating uncertainty in enforcement regarding what the 'Exclusive Category' actually entails. It should also be noted that with this change, it may create an obligation for the parties to 'agree upon' such categories which is not specified in the contract, nor whether they should be on a written record or not.
perturbation.contradicted_text: During the Term, Snap will not grant any third party any right to sponsor any products or services in the Exclusive Category on or through the Snap Web Site. For the avoidance of doubt, the parties acknowledge that the foregoing restriction applies only to persistent sponsorship placement as judged by Sponsor at its discretion, and not to run-of-site banner advertisements or other rotating promotional placements.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the definition of 'EXCLUSIVE CATEGORY' from a clearly defined list in Exhibit A to something 'as agreed upon by both parties.' This introduces ambiguity and uncertainty. The contradicted text, Section 2.6, relies on the existence of a defined 'Exclusive Category' to restrict Snap from granting sponsorship rights to third parties. By removing the clear reference to Exhibit A, the contract now lacks a concrete definition of what constitutes the 'Exclusive Category,' making it difficult to enforce Section 2.6. The lack of a clear definition directly contradicts the requirement in the contradicted section that Snap will not grant any third party any right to sponsor any products or services 'in the Exclusive Category.' Because the exclusive category is now undefined and must be 'agreed upon,' it creates an omission in the contract and leads to an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text:
EXHIBIT A

PAYMENTS AND EXCLUSIVITY

1. The Exclusive Category is "PROVIDERS OF TRAVEL SERVICE".
2. The up-front payment called for by Section 3.1 will be [*].
3. Sponsor shall pay the following non-accrued obligation quarterly fees on or prior to the following dates:

June 30, 1999 [*]
September 30, 1999 [*]
December 31, 1999 [*]
March 31, 2000 [*]
June 30, 2000 [*]
September 30, 2000 [*]
December 31, 2000 [*]
perturbation.changed_text:
EXHIBIT A

PAYMENTS

2. The up-front payment called for by Section 3.1 will be [*].
3. Sponsor shall pay the following non-accrued obligation quarterly fees on or prior to the following dates:

June 30, 1999 [*]
September 30, 1999 [*]
December 31, 1999 [*]
March 31, 2000 [*]
June 30, 2000 [*]
September 30, 2000 [*]
December 31, 2000 [*]
perturbation.explanation: By removing the sentence defining the 'Exclusive Category' in Exhibit A, we create a contradiction with Section 1 and Section 2.6 where the term is used. Section 1 defines Exclusive Category as being identified on Exhibit A which is not the case anymore, creating an internal contradiction since Exhibit A does not contain the category anymore, creating uncertainty in enforcement regarding what the 'Exclusive Category' actually entails. Also, since this is one of the most important definitions in the contract. This creates uncertainty, who can Sponsor advertise with.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Wherever used in this Agreement with initial terms capitalized, the following terms shall have the following defined meanings: "CO-BRANDED PAGES" means pages of the Snap Web Site, to be developed pursuant to Section 2.2 [CO-BRANDED PAGES] and hosted on the Snap Web Site, that display both the Snap Brand Features and the Sponsor Brand Features, along with such Sponsor Content, Snap Content and other content as the parties may agree upon. "CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION" means information of either party (whether of a technical, business or other nature) which the other party knows or reasonably should know to be confidential or proprietary information of such party. "INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY RIGHTS" means any patent, copyright, rights in Trademarks, trade secret rights, moral rights and other intellectual property or proprietary rights arising under the laws of any jurisdiction. "EXCLUSIVE CATEGORY" means those product and/or service categories identified on Exhibit A. "SNAP BRAND FEATURES" means the Snap Marks and Snap's distinct brand elements that appear from time to time in Snap's properties, ventures and services worldwide and are protected under U.S. copyright law or for which Snap has established trademark, service mark or trade dress rights and any modifications to the foregoing that may be created during the term of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text defined the 'Exclusive Category' in Exhibit A, but the changed text removes this definition. The contradicted text defines 'EXCLUSIVE CATEGORY' as being identified on Exhibit A. Because Exhibit A no longer contains this definition after the modification, the changed text directly contradicts the definition provided in the contradicted text, creating ambiguity and uncertainty regarding the scope and meaning of the 'Exclusive Category' as used elsewhere in the agreement, leading to a strong in-text contradiction, fitting the definition of 'Omissions - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: In addition, Sponsor will perform those specific promotional and marketing services specified on Exhibit D.
perturbation.changed_text: In addition, Sponsor will perform those specific promotional and marketing services in accordance with industry standards.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the reference to Exhibit D, where specific promotional and marketing services are detailed, and replacing it with a vague standard of 'industry standards', we create an in-text contradiction. Section 2.5 now mentions promotional services without clearly defining what those are, while Exhibit D no longer contains the marketing specifics initially promised. Without the specifics outlined in Exhibit D, it becomes unclear what Sponsor is obligated to do, making Section 2.5 difficult to enforce and opens the door to disputes about what constitutes 'industry standards'.
perturbation.contradicted_text:
SPONSOR PROMOTIONAL ACTIVITIES

Sponsor and Snap shall work together to create mutually acceptable content for inclusion on the "Going to School" section of the Snap Web Site. On each page of the Sponsor Web Site linked to or from the Co-Branded Pages or containing any Snap Content, Sponsor shall include, in a mutually acceptable "above-the-fold" location (based on a 640x480 pixels screen), a logo link containing the Snap Brand Features, to be supplied by Snap, which links back to the Snap Web Site. Sponsor shall make available to Snap [*] of advertising placements (based on Sponsor's standard prices for the same or similar advertising) during each the calendar years 1999 and 2000 in marketing vehicles owned or controlled by Sponsor as reasonably requested by Snap for the purpose of marketing the Co-Branded Pages and the services provided on such pages. The parties acknowledge that Snap has elected to use HEMISPHERES magazine as the initial marketing vehicle. Snap acknowledges that marketing pieces included in Sponsor's marketing vehicles must be reasonably consistent with the overall message delivered by such vehicle. Sponsor shall develop a its new proprietary product category to be offered through the Co-Branded Pages that shall represent a college-oriented electronic fare based on Sponsor's E-Fare-Registered Trademark- product. It is the parties' expectations that the investment required of Sponsor to deliver this new product category to the relationship described in this Agreement shall be approximately [*]. Sponsor shall make available to Snap [*] worth of travel on Sponsor's airline ([*] in calendar 1999 and [*] in calendar 2000) to be given away by Snap through a sweepstakes process as a promotional tool. Snap shall be free to elect to give away domestic or international travel in its sole election and will be credited [*] per domestic roundtrip ticket given away and [*] per international roundtrip ticket given away. In the event that the parties mutually determine that the sweepstakes form of promotion is a valuable marketing tool, the parties may agree to make additional tickets available to be given away.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly referred to Exhibit D for specific promotional and marketing services. The changed text replaces this reference with a vague reference to 'industry standards.' This creates a contradiction because the contradicted text describes specific promotional activities like content inclusion on the Snap Web Site, logo links, and advertising placements. By removing the explicit reference to Exhibit D and replacing it with 'industry standards,' the changed text makes it unclear what promotional activities are required, potentially conflicting with and undermining the specific promotional activities detailed in the contradicted text. The reliance on 'industry standards' lacks the specificity required to align with the detailed activities outlined in the contradicted section, causing uncertainty about the Sponsor's obligations. Thus, a strong in-text contradiction is introduced.
